question_text: The ABO blood group system in humans was discovered by:
explanation: Karl Landsteiner discovered the ABO blood group system in 1900-1901. He observed that the serum of one individual could agglutinate (clump) the red blood cells of another individual. Based on the presence or absence of two antigens (A and B) on the surface of RBCs, he classified human blood into groups A, B, and O. The fourth group AB was discovered later by Decastello and Sturli (1902). Landsteiner was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1930 for this groundbreaking discovery. This discovery made safe blood transfusions possible and laid the foundation for the science of immunohaematology.

elimination_hints.hint: William Harvey is famous for discovering the systemic circulation of blood (1628), not blood groups. His work explained how blood flows through the body, not its immunological classification.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the discovery of blood circulation with blood grouping

elimination_hints.hint: Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin (the first antibiotic) in 1928. He worked in bacteriology, not haematology.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up Nobel Prize-winning medical discoveries

elimination_hints.hint: Robert Koch is known for Koch's postulates and the discovery of the tuberculosis and anthrax bacilli. He contributed to microbiology, not blood group serology.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing pioneers of microbiology with those of immunohaematology

question_text: A person with blood group AB has:
explanation: In the ABO blood group system, blood group AB individuals possess both antigen A and antigen B on the surface of their red blood cells (RBCs). These antigens are glycoproteins/glycolipids present on the RBC membrane. Importantly, AB individuals do NOT produce anti-A or anti-B antibodies in their plasma. This is because the immune system develops tolerance to self-antigens during fetal development. Since AB individuals lack both antibodies, they can receive blood from donors of any ABO group (A, B, AB, or O) without agglutination, making them 'universal recipients'. The antigens are genetically determined: genotype I^A I^B produces both antigens due to codominance.

options.text: Antigen A and antibody anti-B in plasma
Antigen B and antibody anti-A in plasma
Both antigens A and B but no antibodies anti-A or anti-B
No antigens but both antibodies anti-A and anti-B

elimination_hints.hint: This describes blood group A, not AB. In group A, antigen A is present on RBCs and anti-B antibody is in the plasma. Group AB has BOTH antigens and NO antibodies.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the antigen-antibody profile of blood group A with blood group AB

elimination_hints.hint: This describes blood group B. In group B, antigen B is on RBCs and anti-A antibody is in plasma. Group AB has both antigens and neither antibody.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the antigen-antibody profile of blood group B with blood group AB

elimination_hints.hint: This describes blood group O, not AB. Group O has no A or B antigens on RBCs but has both anti-A and anti-B antibodies in plasma. Group AB is the exact opposite.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the antigen-antibody profiles of groups O and AB

question_text: Which blood group is called the 'universal donor'?
explanation: Blood group O is called the universal donor because O-type RBCs lack both A and B antigens on their surface. When O-type blood is transfused into a recipient of any ABO blood group (A, B, AB, or O), the recipient's antibodies (anti-A, anti-B, or both) find no corresponding antigens on the donated RBCs to cause agglutination. Therefore, O-type blood does not trigger an immune response in any recipient. However, it is important to note that this concept applies mainly to packed RBC transfusions. Whole blood from group O contains anti-A and anti-B antibodies in its plasma, which could theoretically react with the recipient's RBCs, but in practice the donor antibodies get diluted in the recipient's blood volume and rarely cause significant problems. In modern medicine, cross-matching is still mandatory before any transfusion.

elimination_hints.hint: Blood group A has antigen A on its RBCs. If transfused into a person with blood group B or O (who have anti-A antibodies), agglutination will occur. So group A cannot donate to everyone.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting that group A RBCs carry antigen A which reacts with anti-A antibodies

elimination_hints.hint: Blood group B has antigen B on its RBCs. If given to a person with group A or O (who carry anti-B antibodies), the transfused RBCs will be agglutinated.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting that group B RBCs carry antigen B which causes agglutination in incompatible recipients

elimination_hints.hint: Blood group AB has BOTH antigens A and B. It would be agglutinated by anti-A and/or anti-B antibodies in nearly every other blood group. AB is the universal RECIPIENT, not the universal donor.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing universal donor (O) with universal recipient (AB)

question_text: Agglutination during blood transfusion occurs due to the reaction between:
explanation: Agglutination (clumping of red blood cells) occurs when antigens present on the surface of donor RBCs react with the corresponding antibodies (agglutinins) present in the recipient's plasma. For example, if a person with blood group A (who has anti-B antibodies) receives blood from a group B donor (whose RBCs carry antigen B), the anti-B antibodies in the recipient's plasma will bind to the B antigens on the donated RBCs, causing them to clump together. This antigen-antibody reaction is the basis of ABO blood group incompatibility. The clumped RBCs can block blood vessels, undergo haemolysis, and release toxic substances, leading to potentially fatal transfusion reactions.

options.text: Antigens of donor and antigens of recipient
Antibodies of donor and antibodies of recipient
Antigens of recipient and antibodies of donor
Antigens of donor and antibodies of recipient

elimination_hints.hint: Antigens do not react with other antigens. Agglutination is an antigen-antibody reaction — antibodies in the recipient's plasma bind to antigens on donor RBCs.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking antigens can directly interact with each other to cause clumping

elimination_hints.hint: Antibodies do not react with other antibodies. Agglutination requires an antigen-antibody binding event. Antibodies are specific to antigens, not to other antibodies.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing antibody-antibody interaction with antigen-antibody interaction

elimination_hints.hint: In standard ABO transfusion reactions, the primary concern is the recipient's antibodies reacting with the DONOR's RBC antigens, not the other way around. Donor antibodies get diluted in the recipient's larger blood volume.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the roles of donor and recipient in the agglutination reaction

question_text: The inheritance of ABO blood groups in humans is an example of:
explanation: The ABO blood group system is a classic example of codominance and multiple allelism. Three alleles — I^A, I^B, and i — control ABO blood group inheritance, but any individual carries only two of these alleles (one from each parent). This is multiple allelism (more than two alleles exist in the population for a single gene locus). When both I^A and I^B are present together (genotype I^A I^B), both are expressed equally — the RBCs carry both A and B antigens. This simultaneous, independent expression of both alleles is codominance. Neither allele is dominant over the other; both produce their respective antigens. This is different from incomplete dominance, where the phenotype is a blend. Both I^A and I^B are dominant over i (the recessive allele that produces no antigen).

elimination_hints.hint: In incomplete dominance, the heterozygous phenotype is an intermediate BLEND of the two parental phenotypes (e.g., pink from red x white in snapdragons). In ABO blood groups, both A and B antigens are fully expressed in AB individuals — there is no blending.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing codominance (both alleles fully expressed) with incomplete dominance (blended phenotype)

elimination_hints.hint: Epistasis involves one gene masking the expression of another gene at a different locus. ABO blood groups involve alleles of the SAME gene interacting, not two different genes masking each other.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing allelic interactions (codominance) with non-allelic gene interactions (epistasis)

elimination_hints.hint: Polygenic inheritance involves multiple genes contributing additively to a single trait (e.g., skin colour, height). ABO blood group is controlled by a single gene with multiple alleles, not by many genes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing multiple alleles of one gene with multiple genes controlling one trait

question_text: A mother has blood group O and the father has blood group AB. Which of the following blood groups is NOT possible in their offspring?
explanation: Mother with blood group O has the genotype ii (homozygous recessive). Father with blood group AB has the genotype I^A I^B. In a cross: ii x I^A I^B, the possible offspring genotypes are I^A i (blood group A) and I^B i (blood group B). Each parent contributes one allele — the mother can only give i, while the father gives either I^A or I^B. Therefore, the children can ONLY be blood group A or blood group B. Blood group O (genotype ii) is not possible because the father cannot contribute an i allele. Blood group AB (genotype I^A I^B) is also not possible because the mother cannot contribute I^A or I^B. This cross will produce children with blood group A and blood group B in a 1:1 ratio.

elimination_hints.hint: Blood group A (genotype I^A i) IS possible. The father contributes I^A and the mother contributes i, resulting in I^A i which is blood group A.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting that the father (AB) can pass on the I^A allele to offspring

elimination_hints.hint: Blood group B (genotype I^B i) IS possible. The father contributes I^B and the mother contributes i, resulting in I^B i which is blood group B.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting that the father (AB) can pass on the I^B allele to offspring

elimination_hints.hint: Blood groups A and B are the ONLY groups that ARE possible from this cross. The question asks which groups are NOT possible.
elimination_hints.misconception: Misreading the question — selecting the possible outcomes instead of the impossible ones

question_text: Erythroblastosis fetalis can occur when:
explanation: Erythroblastosis fetalis (Haemolytic Disease of the Newborn, HDN) occurs when an Rh-negative (Rh−) mother carries an Rh-positive (Rh+) fetus. During the first pregnancy with an Rh+ baby, some fetal Rh+ RBCs may enter the mother's blood during delivery. The mother's immune system recognises the Rh antigen as foreign and produces anti-Rh antibodies (IgG class). These antibodies persist in the mother's blood. During a subsequent pregnancy with another Rh+ fetus, the maternal anti-Rh antibodies (IgG, which can cross the placenta) enter the fetal circulation and attack the fetal Rh+ RBCs, causing their agglutination and haemolysis. This leads to severe anaemia, jaundice, and oedema (hydrops fetalis) in the newborn. The condition is prevented by administering anti-Rh antibodies (RhoGAM) to the mother within 72 hours after the first delivery.

options.text: An Rh-negative mother carries an Rh-positive fetus
An Rh-positive mother carries an Rh-negative fetus
An Rh-positive mother carries an Rh-positive fetus
An Rh-negative mother carries an Rh-negative fetus

elimination_hints.hint: An Rh+ mother already has Rh antigens and therefore will NOT produce anti-Rh antibodies. It is only an Rh− mother who lacks the Rh antigen and can mount an immune response against Rh+ fetal RBCs.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the Rh status of mother and fetus required for erythroblastosis fetalis

elimination_hints.hint: If both mother and fetus are Rh+, there is no Rh incompatibility. The mother's immune system sees the Rh antigen as self and does not produce anti-Rh antibodies.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking Rh incompatibility can occur even when mother and fetus share the same Rh status

elimination_hints.hint: If both mother and fetus are Rh−, neither has the Rh antigen, so there is no foreign antigen to trigger an immune response. No anti-Rh antibodies are formed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking erythroblastosis fetalis can occur without any Rh antigen mismatch

question_text: A person with genotype I^A i will have blood group:
explanation: In ABO blood group genetics, the allele I^A codes for the enzyme that adds N-acetylgalactosamine to the H-antigen on RBCs, producing antigen A. The allele i is recessive and does not produce any modifying enzyme, so the H-antigen remains unmodified (no A or B antigen). When a person has the genotype I^A i, the dominant I^A allele is expressed, and antigen A is produced on the RBC surface. The recessive i allele does not contribute any antigen. Therefore, the phenotype is blood group A. This person is heterozygous for blood group A. The plasma will contain anti-B antibodies (since the person lacks antigen B). A homozygous blood group A individual would have the genotype I^A I^A.

elimination_hints.hint: Blood group O requires the genotype ii (homozygous recessive). The presence of even one I^A allele will produce antigen A, giving blood group A, not O.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking the recessive i allele can mask the dominant I^A allele

elimination_hints.hint: Blood group B requires at least one I^B allele (genotype I^B I^B or I^B i). The genotype I^A i has no I^B allele, so antigen B cannot be produced.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing I^A and I^B alleles and their corresponding antigens

elimination_hints.hint: Blood group AB requires both I^A and I^B alleles (genotype I^A I^B). The genotype I^A i has only I^A and the recessive i, so only antigen A is produced.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking the i allele somehow contributes a B antigen

question_text: Cross-matching of blood before transfusion is essential because:
explanation: Cross-matching is a laboratory test performed before blood transfusion to check compatibility between the donor's blood and the recipient's blood. It involves mixing the donor's RBCs with the recipient's serum (major cross-match) and the recipient's RBCs with the donor's serum (minor cross-match). This detects antibodies in the recipient's serum that might react with antigens on the donor's RBCs (and vice versa), which could cause agglutination and haemolysis. Cross-matching is essential because ABO and Rh typing alone may not detect all incompatibilities — there are over 30 blood group systems with hundreds of antigens (e.g., Kell, Duffy, Kidd, MNS). A person may have antibodies against minor blood group antigens due to previous transfusions or pregnancies. Cross-matching ensures safety beyond just ABO and Rh matching.

options.text: It determines the volume of blood to be transfused
It measures the haemoglobin level of the donor
It screens for infectious diseases in the donor blood
It detects possible incompatibilities between donor and recipient blood beyond ABO and Rh typing

elimination_hints.hint: The volume of blood to be transfused is determined by clinical assessment of the patient's needs (haemoglobin level, blood loss), not by cross-matching.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing cross-matching (compatibility testing) with clinical dosage decisions

elimination_hints.hint: Haemoglobin levels are measured by routine blood tests (e.g., CBC), not by cross-matching. Cross-matching specifically tests immunological compatibility.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing cross-matching with routine haematological investigations

elimination_hints.hint: Screening for infectious diseases (HIV, Hepatitis B/C, syphilis, malaria) is done through separate serological and molecular tests, not through cross-matching.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing cross-matching with blood screening for pathogens

question_text: The Rh factor in human blood was named after:
explanation: The Rh factor was discovered by Karl Landsteiner and Alexander Wiener in 1940. They injected blood from the Rhesus monkey (Macaca mulatta) into rabbits and guinea pigs. The resulting antibodies produced in the experimental animals could agglutinate not only Rhesus monkey RBCs but also the RBCs of about 85% of human subjects. These individuals were termed Rh-positive (Rh+), while the remaining 15% whose RBCs were not agglutinated were called Rh-negative (Rh−). The factor was named 'Rh' after the Rhesus monkey used in the experiments. The Rh blood group system is the second most important blood group system (after ABO) in transfusion medicine and is critical in preventing erythroblastosis fetalis.

elimination_hints.hint: Rhino beetles are arthropods and have no relevance to human blood group research. The 'Rh' abbreviation specifically comes from 'Rhesus' monkey.
elimination_hints.misconception: Associating 'Rh' with any organism whose name starts with 'Rh'

elimination_hints.hint: Rhizopus is a fungus (bread mould) used in studies of asexual and sexual reproduction. It has no connection to blood group discovery.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing organisms with names starting with 'Rh'

elimination_hints.hint: Rhinoceros is a large mammal, but it was never used in blood group experiments. The Rhesus monkey (Macaca mulatta) was the specific primate used by Landsteiner and Wiener.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly associating 'Rh' with another 'Rh-' named animal

question_text: In a family, the father is heterozygous for blood group A and the mother is heterozygous for blood group B. What is the probability that their child will be a universal donor?
explanation: Father is heterozygous for blood group A, so his genotype is I^A i. Mother is heterozygous for blood group B, so her genotype is I^B i. Cross: I^A i x I^B i. Using a Punnett square — Father's gametes: I^A and i; Mother's gametes: I^B and i. Offspring genotypes: I^A I^B (blood group AB), I^A i (blood group A), I^B i (blood group B), and ii (blood group O). Each genotype occurs with a probability of 1/4. The universal donor is blood group O (genotype ii). Therefore, the probability that the child will be a universal donor is 1/4 (25%). This cross is significant because it can produce offspring of ALL FOUR blood groups — A, B, AB, and O — making it a favourite question in genetics.

elimination_hints.hint: 1/2 would be the probability if one parent were Ii and the other were ii (test cross). In this cross (I^A i x I^B i), four distinct genotypes are produced in equal proportions, so each has a 1/4 probability.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly simplifying the dihybrid-like cross to a monohybrid ratio

elimination_hints.hint: 3/4 would represent the combined probability of A + B + AB offspring. Only the ii genotype (1/4) gives blood group O, the universal donor.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the probability of expressing any dominant phenotype with the probability of the recessive phenotype

elimination_hints.hint: 1/8 is not obtainable from a simple cross between two heterozygous parents at a single locus. The Punnett square for I^A i x I^B i gives four equally likely outcomes, each with probability 1/4.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly applying dihybrid cross probabilities to a single-locus problem

question_text: A couple has three children with blood groups A, B, and O. A medicolegal case questions the paternity of the child with blood group O. Based on genetics, which statement is correct?
explanation: For a couple to produce children with blood groups A, B, and O, we must determine what parental genotypes allow all three phenotypes. Blood group O requires genotype ii, meaning both parents must contribute an i allele. Blood group A requires at least one I^A allele, and blood group B requires at least one I^B allele. The only parental genotype combination that satisfies all conditions is: one parent I^A i (heterozygous A) and the other parent I^B i (heterozygous B). Cross: I^A i x I^B i produces I^A I^B (AB), I^A i (A), I^B i (B), and ii (O). Since all three observed blood groups (A, B, and O) can be produced from this single parental cross, the child with blood group O is genetically consistent with the same parents. The paternity claim is therefore not supported by blood group evidence alone.

options.text: The parents are most likely I^A i and I^B i, and the group O child is genetically consistent with them
A group O child cannot be born to parents with blood groups A and B
The father must have blood group O for the child to be group O
The child with blood group O must have a different father

elimination_hints.hint: This is incorrect because HETEROZYGOUS group A (I^A i) and HETEROZYGOUS group B (I^B i) parents can both contribute the i allele, producing an ii (group O) child with 25% probability.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming all group A individuals are homozygous (I^A I^A) and all group B are homozygous (I^B I^B)

elimination_hints.hint: The father does NOT need to be group O. He only needs to carry one i allele (be heterozygous I^A i or I^B i). The i allele is recessive and hidden in heterozygotes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting that the i allele can be carried in heterozygous condition without being phenotypically expressed

elimination_hints.hint: The blood group evidence does NOT require a different father. The cross I^A i x I^B i can produce all four blood groups, including O. Concluding different paternity based solely on blood group O is genetically unjustified.
elimination_hints.misconception: Jumping to conclusions about paternity without considering heterozygous genotypes

question_text:
Assertion (A): Blood group O individuals are called universal donors.
Reason (R): The RBCs of blood group O individuals lack both A and B antigens on their surface.
explanation: Both the Assertion and the Reason are correct, and the Reason correctly explains the Assertion. Blood group O individuals are called universal donors precisely BECAUSE their RBCs lack both A and B surface antigens. When O-type RBCs are transfused into a recipient of any ABO blood group, the recipient's plasma antibodies (whether anti-A, anti-B, or both) find no A or B antigens on the donated RBCs to bind with. Since there is no antigen-antibody reaction, no agglutination occurs. This is the direct mechanistic reason why O blood can be safely given to recipients of all ABO groups. The genotype of blood group O is ii — the recessive allele i does not code for any functional enzyme to modify the H-antigen into A or B antigen.

options.text: Both A and R are correct, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct

elimination_hints.hint: The Reason directly explains WHY group O is the universal donor. The absence of A and B antigens is the precise mechanism that prevents agglutination in any recipient. The causal link is direct.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to see the direct cause-effect relationship between lacking antigens and being a universal donor

elimination_hints.hint: The Reason is factually correct — group O RBCs indeed lack both A and B antigens. The genotype ii does not produce any antigen-modifying enzyme.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly thinking group O RBCs carry some antigen

elimination_hints.hint: The Assertion is factually correct — group O IS universally accepted as the universal donor in the ABO system. This is a standard NCERT fact.
elimination_hints.misconception: Questioning a well-established fact about universal donor status

question_text:
Assertion (A): Erythroblastosis fetalis typically does not affect the first Rh-positive child of an Rh-negative mother.
Reason (R): The Rh-negative mother produces anti-Rh antibodies only after exposure to Rh-positive blood, usually during the delivery of the first child.
explanation: Both the Assertion and the Reason are correct, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion. During the first pregnancy, the Rh-negative mother has not yet been exposed to Rh-positive antigens (assuming no prior Rh+ blood transfusion). The fetal and maternal blood supplies are normally separated by the placental barrier. However, during delivery (or miscarriage/abortion), small amounts of fetal Rh+ blood may enter the maternal circulation. This first exposure sensitises the mother — her immune system recognises the Rh antigen as foreign and produces anti-Rh antibodies. However, this immune response is slow (primary immune response) and the antibodies are produced AFTER delivery, so the first child is usually unaffected. During a subsequent pregnancy with an Rh+ fetus, the mother's already-primed immune system mounts a rapid secondary immune response, producing large quantities of IgG anti-Rh antibodies that cross the placenta and attack the fetal RBCs.

options.text: Both A and R are correct, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct

elimination_hints.hint: The Reason directly explains WHY the first child is spared — the mother has not yet been sensitised and has no pre-existing anti-Rh antibodies. The explanation is both causally and temporally linked.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to connect the timeline of sensitisation (after first delivery) with protection of the first child

elimination_hints.hint: The Reason is factually correct. The mother's first exposure to Rh+ antigens typically occurs during the first delivery, triggering antibody production after the child is already born.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking the mother produces anti-Rh antibodies before any exposure to Rh+ blood

elimination_hints.hint: The Assertion is correct — the first Rh+ child is indeed typically unaffected. This is a well-documented clinical observation and an important NCERT concept.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking erythroblastosis fetalis affects the first pregnancy equally

question_text:
Assertion (A): Blood group AB individuals can receive blood from any ABO group, but their blood cannot be safely given to individuals of blood groups A, B, or O.
Reason (R): AB blood contains both I^A and I^B alleles, which produce a unique hybrid antigen that is incompatible with other blood groups.
explanation: The Assertion is correct but the Reason is incorrect. Blood group AB individuals are universal recipients because they lack both anti-A and anti-B antibodies in their plasma. Since their plasma has no antibodies to react with any donor RBC antigens, they can receive A, B, AB, or O blood without agglutination. However, AB individuals cannot donate to A, B, or O recipients because their RBCs carry BOTH A and B antigens — anti-A antibodies in group B/O recipients would attack antigen A, and anti-B antibodies in group A/O recipients would attack antigen B. The Reason is WRONG because I^A and I^B alleles do NOT produce a 'hybrid antigen'. In codominance, each allele independently produces its own distinct antigen — I^A produces antigen A and I^B produces antigen B. Both antigens coexist separately on the RBC surface; they do not fuse into a hybrid molecule.

options.text: Both A and R are correct, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct

elimination_hints.hint: The Reason is scientifically wrong — I^A and I^B produce separate, independent antigens (A and B), not a 'hybrid antigen'. Codominance means both alleles are expressed independently, not that they fuse.
elimination_hints.misconception: Misunderstanding codominance as producing a blended or hybrid product

elimination_hints.hint: Since the Reason is factually incorrect (no hybrid antigen exists), option B is not valid. The Reason must be true for option B to apply.
elimination_hints.misconception: Accepting the 'hybrid antigen' claim as true without critically evaluating it

elimination_hints.hint: The Assertion is factually correct — AB individuals are universal recipients but cannot safely donate to A, B, or O individuals. The Reason is what is wrong here.
elimination_hints.misconception: Questioning the well-established fact that AB is the universal recipient

question_text:
Match the blood group in Column A with the correct antigen(s) on RBCs and antibody(ies) in plasma in Column B.

Column A:
(i) Blood group A
(ii) Blood group B
(iii) Blood group AB
(iv) Blood group O

Column B:
(p) Antigen A and B; No antibodies
(q) Antigen A; Anti-B antibody
(r) No antigens; Anti-A and Anti-B antibodies
(s) Antigen B; Anti-A antibody
explanation: The correct matching is: (i)-q, (ii)-s, (iii)-p, (iv)-r. Blood group A: RBCs carry antigen A; plasma contains anti-B antibodies (q). Blood group B: RBCs carry antigen B; plasma contains anti-A antibodies (s). Blood group AB: RBCs carry both antigens A and B; plasma contains NO antibodies — no anti-A, no anti-B (p). Blood group O: RBCs carry NO antigens (neither A nor B); plasma contains BOTH anti-A and anti-B antibodies (r). The key principle is that the antibodies in plasma are always directed against the antigen(s) ABSENT from the person's own RBCs. This prevents self-agglutination and is a fundamental concept in ABO blood group immunology.

elimination_hints.hint: This incorrectly matches blood group B with (p) — both antigens and no antibodies. Group B has only antigen B and carries anti-A antibodies. The profile with both antigens and no antibodies belongs to group AB.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the antigen-antibody profile of blood group B with blood group AB

elimination_hints.hint: This reverses groups A and B. Group A has antigen A and anti-B (q), not antigen B and anti-A (s). Similarly, group B has antigen B and anti-A (s), not antigen A and anti-B (q).
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping the antigen-antibody profiles of blood groups A and B

elimination_hints.hint: This assigns no antigens and both antibodies (r) to group A, which is incorrect. Group A has antigen A. The profile (r) belongs to group O, which lacks both antigens.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assigning the blood group O profile to blood group A

question_text:
Match the genotype in Column A with the correct blood group phenotype in Column B.

Column A:
(i) I^A I^B
(ii) I^A i
(iii) I^B I^B
(iv) ii

Column B:
(p) Blood group O
(q) Blood group AB
(r) Blood group B
(s) Blood group A
explanation: The correct matching is: (i)-q, (ii)-s, (iii)-r, (iv)-p. Genotype I^A I^B: Both I^A and I^B are codominant — both antigens A and B are expressed, giving blood group AB (q). Genotype I^A i: I^A is dominant over i — only antigen A is expressed, giving blood group A (s). Genotype I^B I^B: Homozygous for I^B — antigen B is expressed, giving blood group B (r). Genotype ii: Homozygous recessive — no A or B antigens are produced, giving blood group O (p). Key points: I^A and I^B are codominant to each other but both are dominant over i. There are six possible genotypes (I^A I^A, I^A i, I^B I^B, I^B i, I^A I^B, ii) but only four phenotypes (A, B, AB, O).

elimination_hints.hint: This matches I^A I^B with blood group A (s) and I^A i with AB (q). I^A I^B must give AB (both antigens expressed by codominance), and I^A i gives group A (I^A dominant over i).
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping the phenotypes of heterozygous I^A i with codominant I^A I^B

elimination_hints.hint: This matches I^A i with blood group B (r) and I^B I^B with blood group A (s). I^A i expresses antigen A (not B), and I^B I^B expresses antigen B (not A). The A and B assignments are reversed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing which allele (I^A or I^B) produces which antigen (A or B)

elimination_hints.hint: This matches I^A I^B with blood group B (r) and I^B I^B with AB (q). I^A I^B always produces BOTH antigens (AB), while I^B I^B produces only antigen B.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking homozygous I^B I^B produces both antigens instead of codominant I^A I^B

question_text: Statement: A person with Rh-positive blood can safely receive Rh-negative blood in the ABO-compatible group without any risk of Rh-related agglutination.
explanation: This statement is TRUE. An Rh-positive (Rh+) person already has the Rh antigen (D antigen) on their RBCs. When they receive Rh-negative (Rh−) blood, the donated RBCs lack the Rh antigen. Since there is no foreign Rh antigen being introduced, the recipient's immune system has nothing new to react against. Furthermore, Rh+ individuals do not naturally produce anti-Rh antibodies (unlike the ABO system where antibodies are naturally present). The Rh incompatibility problem arises only in the reverse situation — when an Rh− person (who lacks the Rh antigen) is exposed to Rh+ blood, which introduces the foreign Rh antigen and can trigger anti-Rh antibody production.

elimination_hints.hint: Rh-negative blood lacks the Rh antigen, so it introduces no foreign Rh antigen into an Rh+ recipient. No anti-Rh response is triggered. Problems only arise when Rh+ blood goes INTO an Rh− person.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking any Rh mismatch causes problems, regardless of direction

question_text: In the ABO blood group system, the alleles I^A and I^B show _____ when present together, as both antigens A and B are expressed simultaneously on the RBC surface.
explanation: The correct answer is codominance. When both I^A and I^B alleles are present together in an individual (genotype I^A I^B), neither allele masks the expression of the other. Instead, both alleles are independently and fully expressed — I^A directs the synthesis of antigen A and I^B directs the synthesis of antigen B. Both antigens are present simultaneously on the surface of every RBC. This phenomenon is called codominance. It is different from incomplete dominance (where a blended intermediate phenotype appears) and from simple dominance (where one allele completely masks the other). The AB blood group phenotype is the textbook example of codominance in human genetics, frequently tested in NEET. Both I^A and I^B individually show complete dominance over the recessive i allele.

elimination_hints.hint: In complete dominance, one allele completely masks the other, and only the dominant phenotype is seen in heterozygotes. In I^A I^B, BOTH alleles are expressed — neither masks the other.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing codominance with complete dominance; in codominance both phenotypes appear, not just one

elimination_hints.hint: Incomplete dominance produces a BLENDED intermediate phenotype (e.g., pink from red x white). In AB blood group, both antigens A and B are FULLY and SEPARATELY expressed — there is no intermediate 'blended' antigen.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing codominance (both fully expressed) with incomplete dominance (blended expression)

elimination_hints.hint: Epistasis involves one gene masking the expression of a different gene at another locus. I^A and I^B are alleles of the SAME gene (ABO locus on chromosome 9) — their interaction is an allelic interaction (codominance), not an intergenic interaction.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing allelic interactions with gene-gene interactions at different loci

question_text:
Scenario: A 28-year-old Rh-negative woman is pregnant with her second child. Her first child was Rh-positive and she did not receive any anti-Rh prophylaxis (RhoGAM injection) after the first delivery. During routine check-ups in the current pregnancy, the fetus is found to be Rh-positive. The doctor warns that the baby is at risk of a serious haemolytic condition.

What is the haemolytic condition the doctor is referring to, and why is the second child at risk while the first child was not affected?
explanation: The condition is erythroblastosis fetalis (Haemolytic Disease of the Newborn). During the first delivery, some Rh+ fetal RBCs entered the mother's Rh− bloodstream. Her immune system recognised the Rh antigen as foreign and produced anti-Rh antibodies (sensitisation/primary immune response). Since this primary response is slow, the first baby was born before significant antibody levels built up and was therefore unaffected. Now, in the second pregnancy with another Rh+ fetus, the mother's immune system is already primed. Exposure to even small amounts of fetal Rh+ antigens (which may leak across the placenta) triggers a rapid and strong secondary (anamnestic) immune response. Large amounts of IgG anti-Rh antibodies are produced, and these antibodies cross the placenta into the fetal circulation. They bind to the Rh+ RBCs of the fetus, causing their agglutination and destruction (haemolysis). This leads to severe fetal anaemia, jaundice (from bilirubin released by RBC destruction), and oedema (hydrops fetalis). Normally, this condition is prevented by administering RhoGAM (anti-D immunoglobulin) to the mother within 72 hours of the first delivery, which destroys any fetal Rh+ RBCs in her blood before her immune system can be sensitised.

options.text: The condition is sickle cell anaemia; the second child is at risk because the Rh-negative mother transmits a defective haemoglobin gene during the second pregnancy

The condition is erythroblastosis fetalis; the second child is at risk because the mother was sensitised during the first delivery and now produces anti-Rh antibodies (IgG) that cross the placenta and attack the fetal Rh-positive RBCs
The condition is thalassemia; the second child is at risk because Rh-negative mothers produce fewer red blood cells during subsequent pregnancies
The condition is ABO incompatibility; the second child is at risk because the mother develops anti-A and anti-B antibodies after the first pregnancy

elimination_hints.hint: Sickle cell anaemia is a genetic disorder caused by a point mutation in the beta-globin gene (HbS). It has nothing to do with Rh factor or maternal-fetal blood incompatibility. It follows autosomal recessive inheritance.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing an immune-mediated haemolytic disease with a genetic haemoglobinopathy

elimination_hints.hint: Thalassemia is a genetic disorder of haemoglobin synthesis (reduced or absent globin chains). It is inherited, not caused by Rh incompatibility or maternal sensitisation. Rh status does not affect globin gene expression.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing Rh-incompatibility-mediated haemolysis with inherited disorders of haemoglobin synthesis

elimination_hints.hint: The scenario specifically describes Rh incompatibility (Rh-negative mother, Rh-positive fetus). ABO incompatibility is a separate issue involving anti-A/anti-B antibodies and typically causes milder disease. The lack of RhoGAM prophylaxis is the key clue pointing to Rh incompatibility.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing Rh incompatibility with ABO incompatibility despite clear scenario cues about Rh status
